claimed_contribution: Introduces a Bayesian simultaneous factor-choice model that jointly estimates CBC choice and binary pick-any brand-image association data to recover willingness to pay for image associations.
Proposes a higher-order confirmatory factor decomposition in which correlated image factors are split into a general brand factor and orthogonal factor-specific residuals.
Shows that conclusions about the value of specific brand-image associations are misleading unless the general brand effect is explicitly controlled.
context: The empirical application is branded midsized sedans, where products carry both parent brand and model names. Data come from a market research study by a major automotive manufacturer with respondents from a large Midwest city in a target segment for near-term midsized sedan purchase.
design_and_data: The study uses 333 respondents who provided binary pick-any associations between five parent brands and six image items (three dependable, three exciting), collected before conjoint tasks. Each respondent then completed 15 CBC tasks with three alternatives per task, plus a five-brand holdout task, with attributes including make/model, engine, audio/navigation, safety features, and price. The authors estimate five Bayesian models (baseline choice only, single-factor, correlated two-factor, and higher-order variants) using MCMC and compare fit with log-marginal density, DIC, and holdout choice performance.
main_findings: A correlated two-factor representation of brand image (dependable and exciting) fits better than a single-factor model for the attitudinal data.
Without higher-order decomposition, high correlation between factor scores creates weak identification and unstable specific-factor WTP estimates, leading to potentially erroneous conclusions.

After controlling for the general brand factor, specific residual image factors show substantial positive WTP (about $4.3k for dependable and $5.39k for exciting per 1 SD change in the best model), while adding the general brand factor to the choice equation does not improve fit and its average WTP interval includes zero.
one_line_summary: The paper develops and applies a Bayesian higher-order simultaneous factor-choice model and finds that specific dependable and exciting brand-image associations have substantial monetary value only after removing a general brand halo factor.
relation_to_prior_work: extends prior latent-variable approaches that combine attitudinal and choice data by explicitly addressing brand-image ratings contaminated by a general brand effect and by estimating WTP for general versus specific image components.
research_gap: Prior work integrating attitudinal and choice data did not directly resolve the brand-image problem that ratings on specific dimensions are often driven by a general brand effect, creating high interitem correlations. This left unclear how much value consumers place on specific image dimensions versus a general brand impression.
research_question: How can consumers' willingness to pay for specific brand-image associations be identified when brand-image ratings are influenced by a general brand (halo) effect? The paper also asks whether the general brand factor itself adds incremental explanatory power for choice beyond heterogeneous brand intercepts and specific residual image factors.
